name: Facebook News Control
description: The theory of 'Facebook News Control' posits that Facebook (Meta Platforms) actively manipulates the news and information presented to its users, influencing public opinion and potentially impacting political outcomes. Proponents argue that this control is exercised through algorithms that prioritize certain content over others, through censorship or suppression of specific viewpoints, and through partnerships with fact-checking organizations that are perceived as biased. The theory suggests that Facebook's actions are not merely neutral algorithmic choices but deliberate attempts to shape narratives and control the flow of information. Evidence cited often includes anecdotal reports of content being flagged or removed, perceived biases in fact-checking processes, and analyses of algorithmic trends that appear to favor certain perspectives. Mainstream perspectives generally acknowledge that Facebook's algorithms can have a significant impact on information dissemination, but attribute this more to optimization for user engagement and advertising revenue rather than deliberate political manipulation. They argue that Facebook faces immense challenges in moderating content fairly and accurately, and that the perception of bias is often subjective and influenced by individual perspectives. Critics of the theory also point out the lack of concrete, irrefutable evidence of intentional manipulation and emphasize the complexity of managing a platform with billions of users and diverse viewpoints. Academic research explores the potential for algorithmic bias and the impact of social media on political polarization, but rarely concludes that Facebook is engaged in a systematic conspiracy to control news.
subtitle: Algorithmic Manipulation and Information Control

claims.claim: Facebook algorithms prioritize specific news sources and viewpoints, creating an echo chamber effect.
claims.evidence: Studies showing algorithmic amplification of certain content, anecdotal user experiences of seeing similar viewpoints repeatedly.
claims.criticism: Algorithms are designed to maximize user engagement, which may inadvertently create echo chambers. Facebook claims to be working on diversifying content exposure.

claims.claim: Facebook censors or suppresses news stories that are critical of specific political actors or ideologies.
claims.evidence: Instances of content being flagged or removed, claims of shadow banning, leaked internal documents (if any).
claims.criticism: Content moderation policies are in place to combat misinformation, hate speech, and harmful content. Mistakes and biases can occur, but are not necessarily indicative of intentional political censorship.

claims.claim: Facebook partners with biased fact-checking organizations to discredit legitimate news sources and promote a specific narrative.
claims.evidence: Criticisms of specific fact-checking organizations, perceived biases in their ratings, examples of disputed fact-checks.
claims.criticism: Fact-checking is intended to combat misinformation and provide users with accurate information. Facebook relies on third-party fact-checkers to avoid bias, but acknowledges that disagreements and controversies can arise.

keyFigures.name: Mark Zuckerberg
keyFigures.role: CEO of Meta Platforms (Facebook's parent company)

keyFigures.name: Sharyl Attkisson
keyFigures.role: Investigative journalist critical of mainstream media narratives and perceived Facebook censorship.

keyFigures.name: Brendan Nyhan
keyFigures.role: Professor studying misinformation and political polarization on social media

mythologyConnections: The concept of a 'gatekeeper' controlling information flow.
Echo and Narcissus - reflecting back only what one wants to see

timeline.event: Concerns arise about Russian interference in the US election via Facebook.
Cambridge Analytica scandal raises concerns about data privacy and manipulation.
Continued debates about Facebook's content moderation policies and algorithmic bias.

sources.author: Pariser, Eli
sources.work: The Filter Bubble: What the Internet Is Hiding from You
sources.title: Pariser, Eli

sources.author: Allcott, Hunt and Gentzkow, Matthew
sources.work: Social Media and Fake News in the 2016 Election
sources.title: Allcott, Hunt and Gentzkow, Matthew

sources.author: Various
sources.work: Articles and Reports from Media Bias/Fact Check
sources.title: Various

worshipPractices.name: Algorithm Divination
worshipPractices.description: Followers meticulously analyze Facebook's algorithm updates, seeking to understand the will of the platform and predict future content trends. They believe that understanding the algorithm is key to appeasing the 'Meta-Gods' and ensuring their messages reach a wider audience. This practice often involves reverse-engineering techniques and statistical analysis.

worshipPractices.name: Content Optimization Rituals
worshipPractices.description: These rituals involve carefully crafting posts and campaigns that adhere to perceived algorithmic preferences. Adherents believe that by using specific keywords, image formats, and posting schedules, they can increase the visibility and engagement of their content, earning favor with the platform.

festivals.name: The Great Unfollowing
festivals.timing: Annually, following major platform algorithm updates
festivals.description: A period of intense scrutiny and mass unfollowing of accounts deemed to be spreading misinformation or unduly influenced by the algorithm. This is seen as a cleansing ritual, purifying the user's feed and re-establishing control over their information diet.

festivals.name: The Data Harvest
festivals.description: A continuous effort to collect, analyze, and disseminate data about Facebook's influence on news and public opinion. This 'harvest' of information is used to inform activism, research, and advocacy efforts aimed at promoting transparency and accountability on the platform.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of 'Facebook News Control' manifests in contemporary discussions about media literacy, algorithmic bias, and the power of social media companies. It fuels ongoing debates about the ethical responsibilities of tech giants and the need for greater transparency in their operations, inspiring movements for digital rights and platform accountability.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Facebook's alleged manipulation of news, Loki uses deception and trickery to sow discord and influence events, often with unforeseen and chaotic consequences for both gods and mortals.

Pheme spreads information, often amplified and distorted, influencing public opinion and causing chaos, similar to how Facebook's algorithms are accused of amplifying certain narratives and suppressing others.

While Thoth is the god of knowledge and writing, he also maintains the records of judgment. The power to record and present information (even selectively) mirrors the alleged control Facebook exerts over the news users see.

Maya, the illusion, veils the true nature of reality. The Facebook News Control parallel lies in the idea that the information presented is a curated and potentially misleading version of reality, distorting perception.

associations.name: Pandora's Box
associations.significance: Opening the 'box' unleashes a flood of curated news, some potentially harmful, with limited ability to control the consequences once released.

associations.name: Smoke
associations.significance: Like smoke, the algorithms obscure the truth, making it difficult to see the source and direction of information manipulation.

associations.name: Hydra
associations.significance: Each attempt to cut off one source of manipulated news leads to the emergence of multiple, making the problem exponentially harder to control.

associations.name: Spider
associations.significance: Facebook, like a spider, weaves a web of information, trapping users within its carefully constructed narrative and controlling their perception of reality.

associations.name: The Fates' Thread
associations.significance: The algorithm, like the Fates' thread, determines the course of information and influences the 'destiny' of public opinion.

associations.name: Lotus
associations.significance: The allure of curated news can be like the Lotus, inducing a state of blissful ignorance and detachment from the unfiltered truth.

associations.name: Midas Touch
associations.significance: Everything Facebook touches turns to gold, but at the cost of genuine connection and authentic information, a hollow, gilded reality.

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: Argus, with his hundred eyes, represents the constant surveillance and data collection inherent in Facebook's algorithms, mirroring the ability to track and analyze user behavior. He provides endless streams of user data.

companions.name: The Sirens
companions.description: The Sirens' alluring songs, irresistible yet dangerous, symbolize the addictive and often misleading nature of clickbait headlines and sensationalized news stories that thrive on Facebook, capturing and holding user attention.

companions.name: Proteus
companions.description: Proteus, the shapeshifting sea god, embodies the ever-changing algorithms and content moderation policies of Facebook, making it difficult to pinpoint and hold accountable any specific source of manipulation.

companions.name: Janus
companions.description: Janus, the two-faced Roman god, represents the duality of Facebook's role, presenting a public face of connecting people while potentially manipulating information behind the scenes. He is the public and private face.

companions.name: The Oracle of Delphi
companions.description: While not directly malevolent, the Oracle's cryptic and often misinterpreted pronouncements mirror how Facebook's algorithms can present information in ways that are open to interpretation and potentially misleading. The Oracle provides the algorithm logic.

extendedContent.title: Echoes of the Oracle: Algorithmic Manipulation and Prophecy
extendedContent.content:
The concept of Facebook News Control finds a chilling parallel in the role of oracles in ancient Greek mythology. Just as Facebook's algorithms act as a filter, selectively presenting information, oracles like the Pythia at Delphi were believed to deliver prophecies shaped by the gods – a form of divine information control. The pronouncements, often cryptic and open to interpretation, were notoriously susceptible to manipulation by priests and political figures, shaping public opinion and influencing the course of events. Like modern algorithms, the oracle's pronouncements held immense power, capable of instigating wars, influencing alliances, and determining the fates of individuals and empires. Consider the story of Oedipus, whose fate was sealed by the oracle's prophecy, driving him toward a tragic destiny he desperately tried to avoid. This illustrates the potential for even seemingly benevolent systems of information dissemination to be used, or misused, to manipulate individual choices and societal outcomes.

The ambiguity inherent in oracular pronouncements mirrors the 'black box' nature of many algorithms. Users are often unaware of the specific criteria used to determine what news they see, making it difficult to assess the fairness or neutrality of the system. The power to curate information, whether divinely inspired or algorithmically generated, carries a profound responsibility. The Delphic oracle, while revered, was also a source of anxiety, a reminder that even seemingly objective sources of knowledge can be subject to bias and manipulation. The anxieties surrounding Facebook News Control echo these ancient fears – that unseen forces are shaping our perceptions of reality.

Ultimately, the legacy of oracles serves as a cautionary tale. Their influence, while profound, was often shrouded in mystery and susceptible to manipulation. Similarly, the perceived control exerted by Facebook over news dissemination raises concerns about transparency, accountability, and the potential for algorithmic bias to shape public discourse in ways that are difficult to detect and even more difficult to counteract. The lesson from antiquity is clear: those who control the flow of information wield immense power, and that power must be wielded with responsibility and a commitment to truth.

extendedContent.title: Censorship as Cerberus: Guarding the Gates of Information
extendedContent.content:
In Greek mythology, Cerberus, the multi-headed hound, guards the gates of the Underworld, preventing the dead from leaving and the living from entering without permission. This image provides a powerful metaphor for the alleged censorship aspect of Facebook News Control. Just as Cerberus selectively allows passage, Facebook is accused of selectively allowing certain information to reach its users, effectively acting as a gatekeeper to the digital realm. The motivations, however, are far from divine; proponents argue that Facebook’s algorithms are driven by profit, bias, or even political agendas.

The three heads of Cerberus can be interpreted as representing the different facets of alleged censorship: the suppression of dissenting voices, the promotion of specific narratives, and the outright removal of content deemed 'inappropriate' or 'harmful.' While legitimate reasons for content moderation exist, critics argue that the criteria used are often vague and inconsistently applied, leading to the silencing of legitimate viewpoints and the creation of an echo chamber effect. This selective filtering of information can have profound consequences, shaping public opinion and influencing political outcomes.

The dangers of unchecked gatekeeping are evident in numerous mythological narratives. The blocking of access to vital resources, the suppression of knowledge, and the silencing of dissent are recurring themes. The story of Prometheus, who was punished for giving fire (knowledge) to humanity, highlights the potential consequences of challenging those who control the flow of information. Similarly, the perceived censorship of Facebook News Control raises concerns about the potential for stifling critical thinking and limiting access to diverse perspectives, ultimately hindering informed decision-making and democratic participation.

extendedContent.title: The River Lethe: Forgetting and the Algorithm's Influence
extendedContent.content:
The River Lethe, one of the rivers of the Underworld in Greek mythology, possessed the power to induce forgetfulness. Souls who drank from its waters lost all memory of their past lives before being reincarnated. This concept offers a compelling parallel to the alleged influence of Facebook's algorithms on shaping public memory and attention. The constant stream of information, tailored to individual preferences, can lead to a collective forgetting of crucial historical events, alternative perspectives, and dissenting voices. Just as the waters of Lethe erase the past, the algorithm can prioritize the present, potentially distorting our understanding of the world.

The rapid pace of information consumption on social media platforms contributes to this phenomenon. News cycles are increasingly short, and attention spans are dwindling. What was once a headline can quickly fade into oblivion, replaced by the next trending topic. This constant influx of information can overwhelm users, making it difficult to retain and process information critically. The algorithm, in its pursuit of engagement, can inadvertently contribute to this collective amnesia, prioritizing sensationalism over substance and novelty over accuracy.

Furthermore, the filter bubble effect, where users are primarily exposed to information that confirms their existing beliefs, can reinforce this forgetting. Alternative perspectives are marginalized, and dissenting voices are silenced, leading to a distorted and incomplete understanding of reality. The River Lethe serves as a potent reminder of the dangers of forgetting, highlighting the importance of critical thinking, historical awareness, and exposure to diverse perspectives in navigating the complex information landscape of the digital age. Just as the souls in the Underworld needed to remember their past lives to learn from their mistakes, we must resist the allure of algorithmic forgetting and actively seek out a more complete and nuanced understanding of the world around us.

extendedContent.title: The Sirens' Song: Algorithmic Enticement and Misinformation
extendedContent.content:
The Sirens of Greek mythology were creatures whose enchanting songs lured sailors to their deaths on the rocky shores of their island. Odysseus famously resisted their call by having his crew plug their ears with wax and tying himself to the mast. This myth serves as a cautionary tale about the seductive power of misinformation and the need for critical thinking in navigating the digital landscape controlled, in part, by platforms like Facebook. Just as the Sirens used their irresistible melodies to deceive sailors, algorithms can be designed to prioritize engagement, often at the expense of accuracy and truth.

The algorithms that govern Facebook's news feed are designed to capture and maintain user attention. This often involves prioritizing sensational, emotionally charged content that is more likely to generate clicks, shares, and comments. Unfortunately, this can also lead to the amplification of misinformation, conspiracy theories, and propaganda. The Sirens' song is analogous to the algorithmic enticement that draws users into echo chambers, reinforcing their existing beliefs and making them more susceptible to manipulation.

The consequences of succumbing to the Sirens' song are dire, as the sailors discovered. Similarly, the consequences of being misled by misinformation can be profound, ranging from political polarization to public health crises. The myth of the Sirens underscores the importance of critical media literacy, the ability to evaluate information objectively, and the willingness to challenge one's own biases. Just as Odysseus had to take extraordinary measures to resist the Sirens' call, we must actively cultivate our critical thinking skills to navigate the treacherous waters of the digital information ecosystem.
